steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The second team hypothesizes that fish sensors are most sensitive exactly where information (pressure difference) is greatest. The study established that for narrower heads, peak pressure difference is closer to the snout; for wider heads, it is farther from the snout. A supporting finding must show sensor sensitivity matching these locations for two fish with different head widths.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — longnose gar (narrower head) has most sensitive sensors closer to snout than devil catfish (wider head): narrower head $\Rightarrow$ sensors closer to snout; wider head $\Rightarrow$ sensors farther from snout. This matches the hypothesis perfectly for both species. ✓
  • Choice B — most sensitive sensors for both are very close to their snouts: places the devil catfish's most sensitive sensors near the snout, but the hypothesis predicts they should be farther from the snout for a wider-headed fish. ✗
  • Choice C — devil catfish all sensors are equally sensitive: if all sensors are equally sensitive, there is no location where sensitivity peaks — this contradicts the hypothesis that sensitivity tracks the location of greatest information. ✗
  • Choice D — most sensitive sensors for devil catfish are closer to snout than those of longnose gar: this reverses the predicted pattern; the wider-headed species should have its most sensitive sensors farther from the snout, not closer. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
